channel.title: Livable Telluride — Daily Digest
channel.description: News, upcoming meetings, and community events for the Telluride region (Town of Telluride, Mountain Village, San Miguel County, and surrounding communities).

item.title: [Event] 2nd Annual Telluride Rotary Hikeathon — May 31, 2026
item.description: 📍 Oak Street Gondola Plaza, Telluride 🕒 11:00 AM kickoff Registration opens April 20 for the 2nd Annual Telluride Rotary Hikeathon. Four weeks of hiking from May 31 through June 28, with a kickoff at 11am on May 31 at the Oak Street gondola plaza. Hike from anywhere and raise funds for the Telluride Rotary Foundation — supporting scholarships, Youth Exchange, international projects, and community grants. 60% of nonprofit team funds go directly back to their nonprofit. Sponsored by Alpine Bank and Jagged Edge Mountain Gear.

item.title: Lawrence de Bivort
item.description: Lawrence de Bivort, who moved to Telluride full-time about a decade ago with his wife Eileen, has passed away. The longtime adventurer climbed fourteeners, foraged for mushrooms, and wrote a book on human cultural evolution. He's survived by his son Benjamin, sister Carlyle, and two nieces and nephews.

item.title: Man charged in Colorado firebomb attack on demonstrators to plead guilty to murder
item.description: A man charged with throwing Molotov cocktails at demonstrators on Boulder's Pearl Street Mall is set to plead guilty to murder charges. An 82-year-old woman died from injuries in the attack and a dozen others were hurt. The suspect faces both state murder charges and federal hate crime charges.

item.title: A late spring snowstorm slams Colorado, closing schools and disrupting commuters
item.description: A late spring snowstorm dumped over 2 feet of snow in mountain areas like Estes Park and up to a foot in Boulder, while Denver got nearly 6 inches. Schools closed Wednesday across the Front Range, giving kids an unexpected May snow day for sledding and playing outside.

item.title: THS left out of soccer playoffs
item.description: The THS girls soccer team missed the state playoffs after losing 2-1 to Bayfield, dropping their ranking from 20th to 21st and out of the top 24 needed to qualify. The Lady Miners finished 4-9-1 after also losing 4-1 to Ridgway earlier in the week.

item.title: Hard work and perseverance
item.description: Darcy Bray graduated last weekend from Colorado Mountain College with her associate’s degree, an achievement she’s been working toward throughout the entirety of her time in high school. Her dedication, perseverance and hard work have truly paid off. Congratulations, Darcy!

item.title: ADL reports a sharp drop in US antisemitic incidents in 2025, driven by a steep fall on campuses
item.description: The ADL reported antisemitic incidents dropped 33% in 2025 to 6,274 cases, largely due to fewer campus incidents, though physical assaults hit a record high of 203. Three people were killed in antisemitic attacks, including two at a DC museum and an elderly woman in Boulder.

item.title: Legals and Public Notices for May 7-13, 2026
item.description: The Planning Commission meets May 14th at 9:30 AM with agenda items including a tabled Garlock Special Use Permit and cancelled Crockett permit application. Mountain Village Town Council passed an ordinance on second reading updating Community Development Code for wildfire resilience compliance.

item.title: Indigenous people honor and raise awareness for relatives who are missing or have been killed
item.description: Indigenous communities marked the day honoring missing and murdered relatives, wearing red to recognize victims of violence. Native Americans face violent crime rates more than twice the general population, with nearly 1,500 active federal missing persons cases.

item.title: Keeping traditions alive
item.description: Norwood's Bill Wilson of Knight Canyon Outfitters has been named "Outfitter of the Year" by the Colorado Outfitters Association for his three-generation family business and work in the West End hunting industry. Wilson and other outfitters are concerned about political threats to hunting rights and what they call "ballot-box biology" - voters deciding wildlife management issues instead of relying on scientific expertise.

item.title: Spring plans meet snow in Denver as a late storm could be the season's biggest
item.description: Denver and the Front Range got hit with what could be the season's biggest snowstorm, with forecasts calling for up to 8 inches in Denver and a foot in Boulder after the driest winter on record. Schools canceled classes Wednesday and thousands bundled up for a David Guetta concert at Red Rocks as temperatures plunged overnight.

item.title: CHALKBOARD for the week of May 7-13
item.description: The weekly community calendar lists birthdays for May 8-13 and regular meeting schedules for town board, school board, and chamber groups. Local activities include the Thursday farmers market through mid-October, senior meals on Mondays and Thursdays, food pantry distribution Sundays, and pickleball sessions multiple days a week.

item.title: You're invited to the groundbreaking of the new Norwood School
item.description: Norwood's holding a groundbreaking ceremony for their new school that's being funded by a $50+ million state BEST Grant and local bonds. The new campus will replace the aging current facilities and consolidate pre-K through 12th grade on a 19-acre site where the old disc golf course was, next to Pinion Park neighborhood and Lone Cone Library.

item.title: Town of Norwood awarded funding for Norwood Hill improvements
item.description: Norwood received $1.25 million in state and federal funding to improve safety on Norwood Hill, which has been a high-priority concern due to its steep grade, sharp curves, and accident history. The project was selected through a competitive statewide process and requires no local matching funds from the town.

item.title: Appreciation for teachers, staff
item.description: The school board passed a resolution officially recognizing Teacher and Staff Appreciation Week for May 4-8, 2026, acknowledging educators' role in student success and academic achievement. The board encourages families and community members to show gratitude to teachers and staff during that week and year-round.

item.title: Trump administration sues Denver over its 1989 assault weapons ban
item.description: The Trump administration filed a lawsuit against Denver's 35-year-old assault weapons ban, claiming it violates Second Amendment rights. Denver's mayor and police chief rejected federal demands to stop enforcing the ban, with Mayor Mike Johnston saying "hell no" to rolling back the policy. The administration is also threatening to sue Colorado over its statewide ban on large-capacity ammunition magazines.

item.title: Telluride community survey open through May 21
item.description: The town's annual community survey is open through May 21, asking residents to rate Telluride's livability across 10 categories including economy, safety, housing, and recreation. Last year's results showed high marks for trails, air quality, and community engagement, but only 1% rated cost of living as good and just 9% said affordable housing was available.

item.title: Sacred shoes
item.description: When Hoka shoes first came out, they were dismissed as cartoon shoes with their big puffy soles, meant to counter the minimalist barefoot running trend promoted by "Born to Run." The author reflects on beloved worn-out shoes being thrown away during spring cleaning, including duct-taped Stinsons and green Vans that had seen basketball games in the Bahamas.

item.title: Boys’ lax blasts into postseason
item.description: The boys lacrosse team crushed Durango 16-2 on Senior Day to win the Mountain League championship and earned the #6 seed for state playoffs. The girls team beat Durango 12-11 in a close rematch after losing to them earlier in the week, with senior Parker Shea scoring five goals. Both teams have moved into state tournament play.

item.title: Man to plead guilty in Colorado firebombing attack on pro-Israel demonstrators
item.description: An Egyptian national who threw Molotov cocktails at pro-Israel demonstrators on Pearl Street Mall in Boulder plans to change his plea to guilty on state charges. The attack killed an 82-year-old woman and injured a dozen others, with the suspect telling investigators he planned it for a year. He faces murder charges in state court and federal hate crime charges, with prosecutors considering the death penalty.

item.title: Telluride CrossFit opens new Lawson Hill gym
item.description: Telluride CrossFit opened their new Lawson Hill location after construction that started last fall and finished in February, with the gym moving in during March. The new space offers more programming flexibility with similar equipment plus additions like wall-mounted rigs, and owner Robin Jones reports membership around 140 people with new offerings including kids' classes and yoga starting in May.

item.title: States across the wildfire-prone Western US are using AI for early detection
item.description: Western states are rapidly expanding AI camera networks to spot wildfires early, with Arizona installing nearly 40 cameras and Colorado's Xcel Energy deploying 126. The technology detects fires about 45 minutes faster than 911 calls, particularly useful in remote areas where blazes might go unnoticed for hours.

item.title: Safe actions in work zones can save lives
item.description: The Transportation Commissioner for District 8 is reminding drivers about work zone safety during National Work Zone Awareness Week in April. CDOT manages around 200 construction projects annually, with workers protected only by plastic cones from passing traffic. Key safety measures include slowing down in work zones, avoiding phone use, and following flaggers' directions, as fines double for speeding violations.

item.title: The heart of Telluride
item.description: Brandon Bermel organized a Valentine's Day street art installation at the Transfer Warehouse called the Heart of Telluride Project, working with Telluride Arts' new director Jessica Galbo. Six local artists created heart-themed pieces for the building's exterior, with community support from Ah Haa School and local businesses providing materials and workspace.

item.title: Rolling in the oats
item.description: A social media influencer has built a following around oat-based recipes, highlighting the ongoing debate between anti-grain advocates and those favoring Mediterranean-style diets that include grains. The writer shares their own history with oats, from surviving on oatmeal during a broke summer in D.C. in 1978 to making fried oat cakes on a Telluride woodstove, noting that while oats are nutritious and technically gluten-free, they can spike blood sugar and contain compounds that may affect some people.

item.title: Jonathan Fouser is Telluride Mountain School’s new head of school
item.description: Jonathan Fouser has been selected as Telluride Mountain School's new head of school after a competitive search that started with 42 candidates. Fouser currently oversees multiple campuses for Brewster American Schools in Spain and has experience founding new schools, which appealed to the board looking for someone to take the mountain school to the next level.

item.title: Town council suspends waitlist policies
item.description: Town council temporarily suspended its waitlist policies for deed-restricted rentals through July 31 to address high vacancy rates. The current system has about 220 people on the waitlist but 39 vacant units (18% vacancy rate), well above the 8% target.

item.title: Sibling stories
item.description: A local bartender at the Vaudeville once worked a shift with his baby in a carrier when a customer warned him that siblings hate each other. That sparked his determination to raise kids who'd be best friends, which mostly worked out through equal treatment, room-sharing, and encouraging fun activities together.

item.title: Paul Wisor resigns as MV Town Manager
item.description: Paul Wisor resigned as Mountain Village Town Manager after participating in an investigation related to the mayor's earlier resignation. Wisor had helped former officials Prohaska and Fee with their controversial offer to purchase Telluride Ski Resort, including providing investor contacts and drafting non-disclosure agreements. He's also been named as a defendant in Telski's lawsuit over the matter.

item.title: Newscast 5-6-26
item.description: Mountain Village Addresses Wildfire with Forest Management; Cat Movie Fisher with Risho Unda; Telluride Yoga Festival Brings Longevity and Service

item.title: New Motor Vehicle Office Availability in Egnar! May 13 at 9 am - 3 pm
item.description: The new Motor Vehicle office will be available in Egnar on May 13 from 9 am - 3 pm in the same building as the fire department (5634 County Rd H1). We hope to serve many community members in this area, eliminating the drive to Norwood or Telluride.

item.title: Newscast 5-4-26
item.description: Paul Wisor Steps Down as Mountain Village Town Manager; General Assembly Enters Its Final Days

item.title: Newscast 5-1-26
item.description: The city of Durango has proclaimed April 19, 2026 as Ross Anderson Day, marking twenty years since the Native American speed skier set a U.S. speed-skiing record of 154.06 miles per hour. We’ll also hear from a researcher working in Bears Ears who is turning to crowdfunding to continue his work after losing a federal grant. Then, we head to Utah, w

item.title: County Planning Commission 5/14 Meeting in TELLURIDE
item.description: San Miguel County • May 1, 2026

item.title: New Wildfire Information Site Launched
item.description: San Miguel County announces the launch of a new wildfire information site, Living with Wildfire. The site is a resource for preparation, mitigation, evacuation and recovery information. More material will be added soon!

item.title: Newscast 4-30-26
item.description: Local Governments Plan for Disaster; West End Roundup with the San Miguel Basin Forum; Cat Movie Fisher with Risho Unda
